top_tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating crossing behavior intentions
Pedestrian crossing behavior under mobile phone distraction
Identify factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
top_datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
28 college students at signalized intersection in Hefei, China
631 valid survey samples collected from six signalized intersections in Hefei, China during May-June 2015
narrative: The appendix records 4 papers and 19 matrix rows used to generate the review synthesis.

The most visible methodological families are Survey-based Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Controlled field experiment, Statistical modeling (Binomial logistic regression), while recurring tasks include Predicting pedestrian violating crossing behavior intentions, Pedestrian crossing behavior under mobile phone distraction, Identify factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections.

evidence_table.title: Effects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor experimental study
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior under mobile phone distraction
datasets: 28 college students at signalized intersection in Hefei, China
claims: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
Pedestrians distracted by music initiate crossing later, increase pupil diameter, and reduce scanning frequency and fixation points toward traffic signal area
Pedestrians distracted by phone conversation cross more slowly, direct fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and spend less fixation time on left traffic area

evidence_table.title: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles considering speed change behavior for the safety assessment of signalized intersections
methods: Discrete choice model with regression analysis
tasks: Pedestrian speed profile modeling, crossing time estimation, vehicle-pedestrian conflict simulation
datasets: Video survey data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya City, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi)
metrics: Modified rho-squared (0.431), Modified R² (acceleration: 0.765, deceleration: 0.725), vehicle yield rate, Post Encroachment Time (PET)
claims: The proposed model represents pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model

The difference between necessary speed to complete crossing and current speed (Vnec - Vcur), conflict area dummy, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase have significant impacts on acceleration/deceleration choices
The pedestrian speed change model provides significantly higher vehicle yield rates compared to constant speed assumptions, demonstrating its importance for safety assessment

evidence_table.title: An extension of the theory of planned behavior to predict pedestrians' violating crossing behavior using structural equation modeling
tasks: Predicting pedestrian violating crossing behavior intentions
datasets: Survey data from 260 respondents in Dalian, China
metrics: RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation)
claims: Instrumental attitude was significant in all four models, indicating pedestrians are willing to take risks to cross the road if this behavior is associated with meaningful benefits like saving time or providing convenience
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility with the best goodness of fit (CFI=0.996, CMIN/DF=1.235, RMSEA=0.030)
Descriptive norm appeared to be a stronger predictor than subjective norm - pedestrians' observations of family and friends' actual behavior had greater influence than perceived approval from them

evidence_table.title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
tasks: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
datasets: 631 valid survey samples collected from six signalized intersections in Hefei, China during May-June 2015
metrics: Score test, odds ratio, p-values, 95% confidence intervals
claims: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, pedestrian's attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards quality of road facility affecting crossing behavior
Strolling pedestrians are more likely to have safe crossing behavior compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio = 0.394)
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, dusk, and night periods
